title: Total Sales shifted to a much higher level in 2021 than in 2020
description: A clear trend break appears in Total Sales over Invoice Date: monthly sales were consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020, indicating a step-change in business scale rather than a short-lived spike. Step 4 explicitly identifies this as a year-over-year level shift, and Step 3 supports it with strong commercial linkage between Units Sold and Total Sales (correlation 0.845) and between Total Sales and Operating Profit (correlation 0.956), suggesting the higher sales level was broad-based and materially meaningful.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports that monthly Total Sales were consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020; Step 3 reports correlation of 0.845 between Units Sold and Total Sales and 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: 2020 monthly Total Sales remained below 2021 levels for most of the period; the pattern is described as a sustained upward level shift across the full year of 2021.

title: Operating Profit followed the same upward regime change, rising sharply in 2021
description: Operating Profit shows a strong trend increase over Invoice Date, with monthly profit totals consistently higher in 2021 than in 2020. This mirrors the sales trend and points to growth that translated into profit dollars rather than revenue alone. Step 4 identifies the shift directly, while Step 3 shows Total Sales and Operating Profit move very closely together with a 0.956 correlation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that monthly Operating Profit shifted to a higher level in 2021 versus 2020; Step 3 reports a 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Monthly Operating Profit in 2021 stayed above 2020 levels on a sustained basis, indicating a structural improvement rather than isolated profit spikes.

title: Units Sold rose to a persistently higher level in 2021
description: Sales volume strengthened materially over Invoice Date: monthly Units Sold were consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020 and stayed elevated through the year. This suggests the growth trend was driven in large part by higher transaction volume. Step 3 reinforces this by showing Units Sold is strongly correlated with Total Sales (0.845) and Operating Profit (0.814).

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports that monthly Units Sold were consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020; Step 3 reports correlations of 0.845 with Total Sales and 0.814 with Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: The pattern is described as an all-year elevation in 2021 monthly unit volume relative to 2020.

title: Total Sales fell sharply in mid-2020, then partially recovered before weakening again
description: Within 2020, Total Sales did not move smoothly upward; instead they dropped from spring into early summer, hit a trough around June, recovered in late summer, and then softened again toward year-end. This creates a clear dip-and-rebound trend pattern rather than steady growth. Step 4 identifies the spring high, June low, late-summer recovery, and weaker finish explicitly.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 describes a sharp decline from spring into early summer 2020, a rebound in late summer, and renewed weakness toward year-end.
data_evidence.data_points: Key turning points cited are a spring high, a June trough, a late-summer rebound, and weaker final months of 2020.

title: Operating Profit also bottomed in June 2020 and recovered later in the year
description: Profitability followed a similar 2020 path to revenue: Operating Profit was relatively strong in spring, fell to a trough in June, improved in late summer, and then weakened again in the final quarter. The parallel movement between sales and profit is consistent with Step 3's 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 notes a spring high, June trough, late-summer recovery, and weaker final quarter for Operating Profit; Step 3 reports a 0.956 Total Sales–Operating Profit correlation.
data_evidence.data_points: The trend includes a mid-year low point in June 2020 followed by a partial rebound before another softening later in the year.

title: In 2021, Total Sales declined through March and then climbed to a July peak
description: The 2021 sales pattern shows a distinct intra-year cycle: Total Sales fell through March, rose strongly from April through July, softened in August through October, and rebounded again by December. This indicates that even in a stronger year overall, demand was not linear and included a mid-year acceleration followed by autumn moderation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies March as the trough and July as the peak for 2021 Total Sales, followed by easing in August-October and a December rebound.
data_evidence.data_points: Turning points explicitly cited are a March low, July high, softer August-October period, and renewed strength in December 2021.

title: In 2021, Operating Profit mirrored sales with a March trough and July peak
description: Operating Profit in 2021 followed the same pattern as Total Sales: it declined through March, rose strongly to July, softened in August to October, and recovered by December. This alignment suggests the mid-year expansion improved profit dollars alongside revenue, not just top-line volume.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies a March trough and July peak in 2021 Operating Profit, followed by softer August-October results and a December rebound.
data_evidence.data_points: The cited 2021 path is decline through March, rise through July, easing in late summer to autumn, then recovery by December.

title: Units Sold in 2021 built from a March low into late summer before dipping in autumn
description: Volume trends in 2021 show a clear build-up pattern: Units Sold were lowest in March, increased through August, then declined in October and November before recovering in December. This indicates that the stronger 2021 performance was supported by sustained volume expansion through most of the year, followed by a temporary autumn slowdown.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that Units Sold were lowest in March 2021, rose through August, dipped in October-November, and recovered in December.
data_evidence.data_points: Named turning points are a March trough, late-summer high period through August, autumn dip, and December recovery.

title: Average Price per Unit was weakest in early 2021 and then trended upward through late 2021
description: Pricing strengthened over the course of 2021: mean Price per Unit fell to its lowest levels in early 2021 and then increased steadily through the rest of the year. This suggests part of the 2021 revenue expansion may have come not only from volume growth but also from improving realized prices later in the year.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports that mean Price per Unit was lower in early 2021 than in 2020 and then rose through late 2021.
data_evidence.data_points: The key pattern is an early-2021 low followed by a sustained upward trend across the remainder of 2021.

title: Online sales likely amplified the overall upward trend because the channel dominates record volume
description: The overall trend toward higher activity over Invoice Date is likely heavily influenced by Online transactions because Step 3 notes that Online dominates sales records. Combined with Step 4's sharp increase in transaction volume at the start of 2021, this suggests the Online channel is a major contributor to the observed trend and should be monitored as the leading indicator of growth.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that Online dominates sales records, and Step 4 reports a sharp rise in monthly record counts at the start of 2021.
data_evidence.data_points: The dataset is described as channel-imbalanced, with Online holding the largest share of observations while monthly transaction counts step up materially in 2021.

title: Foot Locker likely experienced the strongest visible trend impact because it is one of the most represented retailers
description: The broad increase in activity over Invoice Date is likely especially visible for Foot Locker because Step 3 identifies Foot Locker as one of the retailers accounting for a large share of observations. When a retailer has a large record share, the step-up in 2021 transaction volume and higher sales/profit levels are more likely to reflect a sustained retailer-level trend rather than isolated noise.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that Foot Locker and West Gear account for a large share of observations; Step 4 reports a sharp increase in monthly record counts and higher 2021 sales levels.
data_evidence.data_points: The retailer concentration noted in Step 3 implies that major retailers such as Foot Locker likely contributed materially to the 2021 level shift in sales activity.

title: Operating Profit contains the largest concentration of extreme records
description: Operating Profit shows 706 outlier records, representing 7.32% of the dataset, which is the highest outlier count among the core financial measures reviewed. This makes profit anomalies more prevalent than Total Sales outliers (653 records, 6.77%) and Units Sold outliers (627 records, 6.50%), indicating unusually large or unusual profit outcomes are a major exception pattern in the data.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%); 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%); 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%).
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit has the highest outlier share among the high-importance numerical columns assessed.

title: Revenue anomalies are widespread in Total Sales
description: Total Sales has 653 outlier records, or 6.77% of all observations, which is a high anomaly rate for a primary revenue KPI. Because Total Sales is also strongly correlated with Operating Profit at 0.956, these extreme revenue records likely drive a substantial share of the profit anomalies as well.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%); correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Extreme Total Sales values are common enough to materially affect aggregate revenue and profitability analysis.

title: Units Sold anomalies suggest a meaningful set of unusually large-volume transactions
description: Units Sold contains 627 outlier records, equal to 6.50% of the dataset. Since Units Sold is strongly correlated with Total Sales (0.845) and Operating Profit (0.814), these quantity anomalies are likely tied to bulk orders, exceptional transactions, or possible data quality issues that cascade into revenue and profit outliers.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%); correlation with Total Sales = 0.844936119178483; correlation with Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: High-volume records appear frequently enough to distort demand and performance summaries.

title: Price per Unit has relatively few anomalies compared with sales and profit measures
description: Only 81 records, or 0.84% of the dataset, are outliers in Price per Unit. This is far lower than the anomaly rates in Units Sold (6.50%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Operating Profit (7.32%), suggesting that unusual transaction size is more often driven by volume than by extreme pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 81 Price per Unit outliers (0.84%) versus 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%) and 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Pricing exceptions are comparatively rare, while quantity and value exceptions are much more common.

title: Operating Margin is unusually stable despite large anomalies in sales and profit dollars
description: Operating Margin has just 44 outlier records, or 0.46%, even though Operating Profit has 706 outliers (7.32%) and Total Sales has 653 outliers (6.77%). This gap indicates that many extreme dollar-value transactions still follow relatively normal margin structures, making transaction size—not margin distortion—the dominant anomaly pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%) versus 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%) and 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Margin anomalies are rare relative to the much larger number of extreme sales and profit records.

title: Online channel is a likely concentration point for transaction-size anomalies
description: Prior analysis found that Online dominates the sales record count, while quality checks show high outlier rates in Units Sold (6.50%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Operating Profit (7.32%). Because the channel contributes a disproportionately large share of observations, it is a natural hotspot where many of these extreme transaction-size anomalies are likely to occur and should be prioritized for exception review.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Online dominates sales records; outlier rates are 6.50% for Units Sold, 6.77% for Total Sales, and 7.32% for Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: A channel with the largest record volume will tend to contribute the largest absolute count of anomalies when outlier rates are this high.

title: Foot Locker is a likely retailer hotspot for extreme sales and profit records
description: Step 3 shows that Foot Locker is one of the retailers accounting for a large share of observations, while Step 2 identifies substantial anomaly rates in Total Sales (653 records, 6.77%) and Operating Profit (706 records, 7.32%). Given this combination of high representation and elevated global outlier rates, Foot Locker is a plausible concentration area for unusually large transactions that can skew retailer-level comparisons.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Foot Locker accounts for a large share of observations; global outlier counts are 653 for Total Sales and 706 for Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: High-volume retailers are likely to contribute a disproportionate number of extreme-value records.

title: The business shows a structural anomaly between 2020 and 2021 transaction levels
description: Temporal analysis found a clear step-change upward in monthly transaction volume at the start of 2021, with record counts remaining much higher throughout 2021 than in 2020. The same upward level shift appears in Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold, making this a broad structural anomaly rather than a one-metric fluctuation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Monthly transaction volume, Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold all shift to a materially higher level in 2021 versus 2020.
data_evidence.data_points: The anomaly is described as a step-change beginning at the start of 2021 and persisting through the year.

title: Mid-2020 shows an anomalous downturn in Total Sales before recovery
description: Total Sales fell sharply from spring into early summer 2020, reached a trough in June, then rebounded in late summer before weakening again toward year-end. This pattern stands out as an abnormal interruption within the broader sales trajectory and marks mid-2020 as a period of unusually weak revenue performance.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales decline from spring to early summer 2020, hit a June trough, then partially recover.
data_evidence.data_points: The pattern explicitly identifies a sharp mid-2020 drop followed by a rebound and later softening.

title: Mid-2020 profit trough is an anomaly aligned with the sales downturn
description: Operating Profit mirrors the unusual 2020 sales disruption: it starts from a spring high, drops to a June trough, recovers in late summer, and then weakens again in the final quarter. The synchronized trough in both revenue and profit indicates an anomalous profitability shock rather than an isolated accounting fluctuation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit follows a spring high to June trough pattern in 2020, then partially rebounds before weakening again.
data_evidence.data_points: The timing of the profit trough aligns with the Total Sales downturn in mid-2020.

title: March 2021 is an anomalous low point before a strong 2021 rebound
description: In 2021, both Total Sales and Operating Profit decline through March, then rise strongly to a July peak. Units Sold is also lowest in March before climbing into late summer. This makes March 2021 a clear multi-metric anomaly: sales, profit, and volume all simultaneously bottom out before recovering.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 2021 Total Sales trough in March followed by rise to July peak; 2021 Operating Profit trough in March followed by rise to July peak; Units Sold lowest in March before increasing.
data_evidence.data_points: March is the common low point across all three core performance measures in 2021.

title: Total Sales and Operating Profit move almost one-for-one across transactions
description: There is a very strong positive correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit (r = 0.9563), indicating that higher-revenue transactions almost always generate higher operating profit as well. This is the strongest relationship identified in the dataset, so profit performance is tightly linked to sales size at the transaction level.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619, the strongest reported pairwise correlation.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 strong correlations list shows r = 0.9563 for Total Sales vs Operating Profit.

title: Units Sold is strongly associated with Total Sales
description: Units Sold and Total Sales have a strong positive correlation of 0.8449. This suggests transaction revenue is driven substantially by volume, with larger-quantity sales tending to produce much higher sales dollars, even though price variation still leaves the relationship weaker than the sales-profit link.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 strong correlations list reports r = 0.8449 for Units Sold vs Total Sales.

title: Higher-volume transactions also tend to deliver higher Operating Profit
description: Units Sold and Operating Profit are strongly positively correlated (r = 0.8139). This means profit generally rises with sales volume, although the relationship is slightly weaker than Units Sold vs Total Sales, implying that pricing and margin differences also influence profitability.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 strong correlations list reports r = 0.8139 for Units Sold vs Operating Profit.

title: In the Online channel, volume-driven relationships are likely especially important
description: The Online channel dominates record counts according to the statistical summary, while the overall dataset shows strong positive correlations of 0.8449 between Units Sold and Total Sales and 0.8139 between Units Sold and Operating Profit. Because Online contributes a large share of observations, these strong global volume relationships are likely heavily influenced by Online transactions, making unit growth in this channel especially important for revenue and profit expansion.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Online dominates sales records; overall correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 notes channel imbalance with Online dominating records, and reports r = 0.8449 for Units Sold vs Total Sales.

title: Foot Locker likely contributes materially to the strong sales-profit linkage
description: Foot Locker is identified as one of the retailers accounting for a large share of observations, and the overall correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit is extremely high at 0.9563. Given Foot Locker's large presence in the data, this retailer likely contributes meaningfully to the near-linear relationship between revenue and operating profit seen across transactions.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Foot Locker accounts for a large share of observations; overall correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 states Foot Locker/West Gear account for a large share of observations and reports r = 0.9563 for Total Sales vs Operating Profit.

title: Outliers may amplify the observed correlation between sales, volume, and profit
description: The strongest correlations involve Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold, but these same columns also contain notable outlier rates: 6.77% for Total Sales, 7.32% for Operating Profit, and 6.50% for Units Sold. This means the positive relationships are real and strong, but extreme high-value transactions may be making the correlations appear even tighter than they would for a typical transaction.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outliers detected in Operating Profit (7.32%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Units Sold (6.50%), all of which are part of the strongest correlation pairs from Step 3.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 flags 706 Operating Profit outliers, 653 Total Sales outliers, and 627 Units Sold outliers.

title: Sales dollars are far more unevenly distributed than operating margins
description: The dataset shows a strongly right-skewed distribution for both Total Sales and Operating Profit, with means materially above medians, indicating that a relatively small share of records contributes a disproportionate share of dollars. By contrast, Operating Margin is much more stable, with only 44 outlier records (0.46%) versus 653 outliers in Total Sales (6.77%) and 706 in Operating Profit (7.32%). This means typical transactions are much smaller than average revenue and profit figures suggest, while margin behavior is comparatively consistent.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that Total Sales and Operating Profit have means far above medians and strong right tails, while Operating Margin is comparatively stable. Step 2 reports outliers in Operating Profit: 706 records (7.32%), Total Sales: 653 records (6.77%), Operating Margin: 44 records (0.46%).
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier rates: Operating Profit 7.32%, Total Sales 6.77%, Operating Margin 0.46%.

title: Transaction volume is concentrated in a few large-quantity records
description: Units Sold has a noticeably uneven distribution, with 627 records flagged as outliers, representing 6.50% of the dataset. This is consistent with the strong correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales (0.8449) and between Units Sold and Operating Profit (0.8139), suggesting that unusually large-volume transactions are a major driver of both revenue and profit dispersion.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 2 identifies 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%). Step 3 reports correlations of 0.844936119178483 between Units Sold and Total Sales, and 0.8138708043211902 between Units Sold and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Units Sold outliers: 627 records (6.50%); corr(Units Sold, Total Sales)=0.8449; corr(Units Sold, Operating Profit)=0.8139.

title: Revenue and profit move almost one-for-one across records
description: Total Sales and Operating Profit are extremely tightly aligned, with a correlation of 0.9563. This is much stronger than the relationship of either metric with Units Sold, indicating that differences in realized revenue per transaction explain profit variation more directly than volume alone. In comparative terms, profit performance across records is much more similar to sales performance than to unit-volume performance.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 reports corr(Total Sales, Operating Profit)=0.9563074748281619, compared with corr(Units Sold, Total Sales)=0.844936119178483 and corr(Units Sold, Operating Profit)=0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: 0.9563 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit versus 0.8449 and 0.8139 involving Units Sold.

title: Online records dominate the dataset relative to other sales methods
description: The record distribution is not balanced across channels: Online contributes the largest share of observations, clearly exceeding In-store and Outlet. Step 3 explicitly notes that product counts are nearly even across the 6 product groups, but channel mix is skewed toward Online, so comparisons by sales method should account for this imbalance before interpreting raw totals.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that the dataset is operationally balanced across products but not across channels, and that Online dominates sales records.
data_evidence.data_points: Qualitative comparison from Step 3: Online has the highest record count among the 3 sales methods.

title: Product mix is evenly distributed compared with channel and retailer mix
description: Across the 6 product groups, record counts are described as nearly even, which contrasts with the more concentrated distributions seen for Sales Method and Retailer. This suggests that product-category comparisons are less likely to be biased by sample-size imbalance than channel or retailer comparisons.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that product counts are nearly even across the 6 product groups, while Online dominates sales records and Foot Locker/West Gear account for a large share of observations.
data_evidence.data_points: 6 product groups are nearly evenly represented; retailer and sales-method distributions are more concentrated.

title: Foot Locker is one of the most observation-heavy retailers in the dataset
description: Retailer distribution is concentrated rather than uniform, with Foot Locker called out as one of the retailers accounting for a large share of records. Compared with smaller retailers in the dataset, this means Foot Locker-based comparisons are supported by a larger sample and may exert outsized influence on overall patterns.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 notes that Foot Locker and West Gear account for a large share of observations.
data_evidence.data_points: Qualitative comparison from Step 3: Foot Locker is among the top retailers by record count.

title: West Gear also contributes a disproportionately large share of records
description: Like Foot Locker, West Gear is identified as having a large share of observations relative to the rest of the retailer set. This concentration means overall dataset behavior is influenced more by West Gear and Foot Locker than by retailers with fewer records, so retailer comparisons should distinguish between scale effects and true performance differences.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 notes that Foot Locker and West Gear account for a large share of observations.
data_evidence.data_points: Qualitative comparison from Step 3: West Gear is among the top retailers by record count.

title: Price per Unit has the narrowest anomaly profile among core commercial metrics
description: Compared with the other major commercial measures, Price per Unit is relatively tightly distributed, with only 81 outlier records (0.84%). That is far lower than Units Sold at 6.50%, Total Sales at 6.77%, and Operating Profit at 7.32%, indicating that variation in transaction outcomes is driven more by quantity and order size than by extreme pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 2 reports outlier rates of 0.84% for Price per Unit, 6.50% for Units Sold, 6.77% for Total Sales, and 7.32% for Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier counts/rates: Price per Unit 81 (0.84%), Units Sold 627 (6.50%), Total Sales 653 (6.77%), Operating Profit 706 (7.32%).

title: Sales and profit move together in a near-linear pattern across transactions
description: There is a very strong positive pattern between Total Sales and Operating Profit: the correlation is 0.956, indicating that higher-revenue transactions almost always come with higher operating profit. This is one of the clearest structural patterns in the dataset and suggests profitability scales closely with sales dollars, even though Step 2 notes 706 Operating Profit outliers and 653 Total Sales outliers that may amplify the extremes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identified this as the strongest relationship among the numerical variables; Step 2 also flagged 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%) and 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%).

title: Unit volume is the main driver of revenue variation
description: Units Sold has a strong positive relationship with Total Sales, with a correlation of 0.845. This indicates a consistent pattern where larger-quantity transactions tend to generate substantially higher revenue. The relationship is strong but not perfect, implying that Price per Unit also contributes to revenue differences across records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 reported this as one of the three strongest correlations in the dataset.

title: Higher unit volume also consistently translates into higher operating profit
description: Units Sold and Operating Profit show a strong positive pattern with a correlation of 0.814. This means profit generally rises as transaction volume increases, reinforcing that sales scale is a major profitability lever. The pattern is slightly weaker than the sales-profit link, which suggests some variation in profit efficiency across transactions.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identified this as a strong cross-metric relationship, alongside the stronger Total Sales-Operating Profit link.

title: Typical transactions are much smaller than averages suggest
description: Step 3 found that both Total Sales and Operating Profit have means far above medians, creating a strong right-skewed pattern. This means a relatively small number of very large transactions pull the averages upward, so average sales and profit overstate what a typical record looks like. Step 2 supports this with 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%).

data_evidence.key_statistics: Means are far above medians for both Total Sales and Operating Profit, indicating strong right tails.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 flagged 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers, consistent with a small set of extreme high-value records.

title: Operating margin is stable even while sales and profit dollars vary widely
description: A notable pattern in the dataset is that Operating Margin remains comparatively stable despite extreme variation in Total Sales and Operating Profit. Step 2 found only 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%), far fewer than the 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers, suggesting the business maintains relatively consistent profitability rates even when transaction sizes differ dramatically.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin is comparatively stable, while sales and profit show strong right-tail behavior.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 reported 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%) versus 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%).

title: Online records dominate the dataset, indicating a channel concentration pattern
description: Step 3 found that the dataset is not operationally balanced across channels and that Online dominates sales records. This indicates a clear concentration pattern in which one sales channel contributes a disproportionate share of observations relative to In-store and Outlet, which may shape overall transaction behavior and trend interpretation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Channel distribution is imbalanced, with Online dominating record counts.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 explicitly states that product counts are nearly even across 6 product groups, while Online dominates sales records.

title: Product mix is balanced across all 6 product categories
description: Unlike the imbalance seen across channels and retailers, Step 3 found that product counts are nearly even across the 6 product groups. This suggests a stable product-mix pattern where no single Product category overwhelmingly dominates record volume, making cross-product comparisons less likely to be distorted by major count imbalances.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Product counts are nearly even across 6 product groups.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 contrasts balanced product counts with the stronger concentration seen in Sales Method and retailer observations.

title: Foot Locker shows the same retailer concentration pattern seen in the full dataset
description: Step 3 indicates that Foot Locker is one of the retailers accounting for a large share of observations, alongside West Gear. This points to a retailer concentration pattern in which Foot Locker contributes disproportionately to the dataset's transaction records, making it a major driver of overall patterns.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Foot Locker and West Gear account for a large share of observations.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 explicitly names Foot Locker as one of the dominant retailers by record count.

title: West Gear also exhibits a high-record concentration within the retailer mix
description: West Gear is identified in Step 3 as another retailer with a large share of observations. This mirrors the broader retailer concentration pattern and indicates that dataset-level trends are likely influenced heavily by West Gear's transaction activity, not just by a broad, evenly distributed retailer base.

data_evidence.key_statistics: West Gear is one of the retailers with a large share of observations.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 highlights West Gear together with Foot Locker as accounting for a large share of records.
